| Captain               = [[Jamal Bhuyan]]

| First game            = {{fb|BAN}} 2–2 {{fb-rt|THA}}<br /> ([[Kuala Lumpur]], [[Malaysia]]; 27 July 1973)
| Largest win           = {{fb|BAN}} 8–0 {{fb-rt|MDV}}<br /> ([[Dhaka]], [[Bangladesh]]; 23 December 1985)

| Regional cup apps     = 1
| Regional cup first    = [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]]
| Regional cup best     = Group stage ([[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]])

| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 2
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = Quarter-finals ([[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]])
| 3rdRegional name      = [[SAFF Championship]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps  = 13
| 3rdRegional cup first = [[1995 SAARC Gold Cup|1995]]
| 3rdRegional cup best  = '''Champions''' ([[2003 SAFF Gold Cup|2003]])

| 4thRegional cup apps  =

| medaltemplates        =
{{MedalSport|Men's [[Association football|football]]}}
{{Medal|Competition|[[SAFF Championship]]}}
{{MedalGold | [[2003 SAFF Gold Cup|2003 Bangladesh]]|Team}}
{{MedalSilver | [[2005 SAFF Gold Cup|2005 Pakistan]]|Team}}
{{MedalSilver | [[1999 SAFF Gold Cup|1999 India]]|Team}}
{{MedalBronze| [[1995 SAARC Gold Cup|1995 Sri Lanka]]|Team}}
{{Medal|Competition|[[South Asian Games]]}}
{{MedalGold | [[1999 South Asian Games|1999 Katmandu]]|Team}}
{{MedalSilver | [[1984 South Asian Games|1984 Kathmandu]]|Team}}
{{MedalSilver | [[1985 South Asian Games|1985 Dhaka]]|Team}}

{{MedalSilver | [[1995 South Asian Games|1995 Madras]]|Team}}

}}

The '''Bangladesh national football team''' ({{langx|bn|বাংলাদেশ জাতীয় ফুটবল দল}}) is the national recognised football team of [[Bangladesh]] and is controlled by the [[Bangladesh Football Federation]] (BFF), the governing body for [[Football in Bangladesh|football in Bangladesh]], formed in 1972. Bangladesh became member of the [[Asian Football Confederation|Asian Football Confederation]] (AFC)  in 1973 and [[FIFA]] in 1976.

Bangladesh team debuted in 1973 and has yet to qualify for the [[FIFA World Cup]] finals. They were dismissed in the first round of their only [[AFC Asian Cup|Asian Cup]] appearance to date in [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]], by qualifying for it, Bangladesh is one of only two South Asian nations to achieve the feat. The nation's best results came at [[South Asia|subcontinental]] level where it won the [[2003 SAFF Gold Cup]] and were gold medalists at the [[Football at the 1999 South Asian Games|1999 South Asian Games]].

==History==
{{Further|Football in Bangladesh}}

===Emergence (1971–1980)===
Prior to the [[Independence of Bangladesh]], the [[East Pakistan football team]] represented the region in national and international matches beginning in 1948.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Iqbal |first=Ayaz Khan {{!}} Nayir |date=2024-09-22 |title=FOOTBALL: GLORY DAYS, PASS ME BY |url=https://www.dawn.com/news/1860358/football-glory-days-pass-me-by |access-date=2024-09-22 |website=DAWN.COM |language=en|archive-date=8 October 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241008081206/https://www.dawn.com/news/1860358/football-glory-days-pass-me-by|url-status=live}}</ref> The first true precursor to a Bangladesh national football team emerged during the [[Bangladesh Liberation War|1971 Liberation War]] with the formation of the [[Shadhin Bangla football team]], which toured India playing exhibition matches and donated all their prize money to support the [[Provisional Government of Bangladesh|provisional Bangladesh government]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.tbsnews.net/sports/shadhin-bangla-football-team-underappreciated-heroes-172381|title=Shadhin Bangla Football Team: The underappreciated heroes |work=The Business Standard |date=16 December 2020 |access-date=19 November 2021 |archive-date=19 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220319201431/https://www.tbsnews.net/sports/shadhin-bangla-football-team-underappreciated-heroes-172381|url-status=live}}</ref> Following the eight-month war and the eventual surrender of the [[Pakistan Army]] on 16 December 1971, a ''de facto'' national side known as Dhaka XI became active the next year, earning a notable win in [[Dhaka]] against [[Mohun Bagan AC|Mohun Bagan]] on 13 May 1972,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A7%AD-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B7%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AF-%E0%A6%AD%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BF%E0%A7%9F%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F-%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B9%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A8|script-title=bn:৭ কোটি মানুষের জন্য ভালোবাসা নিয়ে ঢাকায় এসেছিল মোহনবাগান |first=Masud |last=Alam |work=[[Prothom Alo]] |language=bn |trans-title=Mohun Bagan came to Dhaka with love for 7 crore people |location=Dhaka |date=19 April 2022 |access-date=19 October 2022 |archive-date=2 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221002192020/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A7%87%E0%A7%AC-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%9F%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A1%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F-%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B9%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A8}}</ref> and finishing as runners-up in the [[Bordoloi Trophy]] held in [[Guwahati]], India, from August 1972.<ref>{{cite book|first=Gautam|last=Roy|title=East Bengal 100|chapter=East Bengal Match Results &ndash; 1972|page=121|date=9 October 2021|publisher=All Sport Foundation|isbn=978-81-947631-0-9}}</ref>

[[File:Bangladesh national football team at the 1973 Merdeka Tournament.png|thumb|left|Bangladesh at the [[1973 Merdeka Tournament|1973 Merdeka Cup]]|alt=File:Bangladesh national football team at the 1973 Merdeka Tournament.png]]

The [[Bangladesh Football Federation]] (BFF) was founded on 15 July 1972 by [[Muhammad Yusuf Ali|Md.Yousuf Ali]], the country's former Minister of Education and Cultural Affairs.<ref>{{cite web |title=BFF info |work=bangladeshdir.com |url=http://www.bangladeshdir.com/recreation-and-sports/football/bangladesh-football-federation-bff/ |access-date=1 January 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180817210435/http://www.bangladeshdir.com/recreation-and-sports/football/bangladesh-football-federation-bff/ |archive-date=17 August 2018 |url-status=dead}}</ref> BFF became affiliated with [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] in 1973 and [[FIFA]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |title=Bangladesh |website=FIFA |url=https://www.fifa.com/associations/association=ban/index.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070616223807/http://www.fifa.com/associations/association=ban/index.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=16 June 2007}}</ref> Former Dhaka XI head coach [[Sheikh Shaheb Ali]] was put in charge of the first national team and [[Zakaria Pintoo]] who led the Shadhin Bangla team before liberation, was made its captain.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.newagebd.net/article/133717/i-am-luckier-than-pele-zakaria-pintoo |title=I am luckier than Pele: Zakaria Pintoo |work=New Age |access-date=18 February 2022 |archive-date=18 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218173354/https://www.newagebd.net/article/133717/i-am-luckier-than-pele-zakaria-pintoo |url-status=live}}</ref> In July 1973, [[Malaysia]] invited Bangladesh to take part in the [[Pestabola Merdeka|Merdeka Cup]], along with Pintoo the first national team consisted of: Shahidur Rahman Shantoo, [[Abdul Motaleb (footballer)|Abdul Motaleb]], Monwar Hossain Nannu, [[Dilip Barua (footballer)|Dilip Barua]], [[Nazir Ahmed Chowdhury|Nazir Ahmed]], Firoj Kabir, Farukuzzaman, [[Abdul Hakim (footballer)|Abdul Hakim]], [[Sharifuzzaman]], [[Mohammed Kaikobad|Md Kaikobad]], Enayetur Rahman, Kazi Salahuddin, [[Pratap Shankar Hazra]], [[Sheikh Ashraf Ali]], [[Sunil Krishna Dey Chowdhury|Sunil Krishna]] and Nowsher.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kironsportsdesk.com/?p=4903 |script-title=bn:১৯৭৩ সালে যাদের হাত ধরে বাংলাদেশ জাতীয় ফুটবল দলের পথ চলা শুরু হয়েছিলো |trans-title=Those who started the journey of the Bangladesh national football team in 1973 |language=bn |website=Kiron Sports Desk |access-date=16 August 2022 |archive-date=27 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220727075458/https://kironsportsdesk.com/?p=4903 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dhakapost.com/sports/football/211015|title=ফুটবলের ৫০ বছর : প্রথম দলের ১৭ জনের কে কোথায় |trans-title=50 years of football: Where are the first team's 17 players?|first=Arafat|last=Zubaear|website=dhakapost.com|date=26 July 2023 |access-date=26 July 2023|archive-date=26 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230726164059/https://www.dhakapost.com/sports/football/211015|language=bn|url-status=live}}</ref>

On 27 July 1973, the Bangladesh football team played its first official game, a 2–2 draw against [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] in the [[1973 Merdeka Tournament|Merdeka Cup]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.kalerkantho.com/print-edition/sports/2020/07/26/939253 |script-title=bn:সেদিন লাল-সবুজ পতাকা উড়িয়েছিল ফুটবল |trans-title=Football flew the red-green flag that day |date=25 July 2020 |work=Kaler Kantho |language=bn |access-date=30 August 2022 |archive-date=30 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220830102857/https://www.kalerkantho.com/print-edition/sports/2020/07/26/939253 |url-status=live}}</ref> [[Enayetur Rahman Khan|Enayetur Rahman]] scored the country's first ever international goal and the second goal was scored by fellow striker [[Kazi Salahuddin]]. After the stalemate, the game went to penalties, where Bangladesh lost 6–5.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/lifestyle/%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%AE-%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B2 |script-title=bn:আন্তর্জাতিক ফুটবলে বাংলাদেশের প্রথম গোল |trans-title=Bangladesh's first goal in international football|work=Prothom Alo |language=bn |access-date=31 August 2022 |archive-date=30 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220830184315/https://www.prothomalo.com/lifestyle/%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%AE-%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B2 |url-status=live}}</ref> On 13 August 1973, after concluding their journey in Malaysia the team played a friendly in Singapore, and earned their first ever win by defeating the hosts 1–0, thanks to a goal from [[AKM Nowsheruzzaman|Nowsher]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%93%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AD%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6 |script-title=bn:নওশেরকে ভুলবে না বাংলাদেশের ফুটবল |trans-title=Bangladeshi football will not forget Nowsher |language=bn |first=Masud |last=Alam |work=Prothom Alo |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=16 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221216065717/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%93%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AD%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6 |url-status=live}}</ref> In August 1975, Bangladesh were again invited to partake in the Merdeka Cup held in [[Kuala Lumpur]], Malaysia. During their underwhelming tournament, the players were informed about the ''[[Assassination of Sheikh Mujibur Rahman]]'', and decided to abandon the tournament to return home amidst the political unrest. However, fearing a FIFA ban the team was obliged to play their game with [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]], making a symbolic protest by keeping the Bangladesh flag at half-mast and wearing a black badge, as they lost 4–0.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.banglatribune.com/national/695981/%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%AE-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6-%E0%A6%B9%E0%A7%9F%C2%A0%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%C2%A0%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87 |script-title=bn:প্রথম প্রতিবাদ হয় মারদেকা কাপে |trans-title=The first protest took place at Merdeka Cup |work=Bangla Tribune |language=bn |access-date=18 September 2022 |archive-date=20 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220920170909/https://www.banglatribune.com/national/695981/%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%AE-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6-%E0%A6%B9%E0%A7%9F%C2%A0%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%C2%A0%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87 |url-status=live}}</ref>

Aside from competing in the [[1976 King's Cup]], Bangladesh remained largely inactive until 1978, when [[Werner Bickelhaupt]] was appointed as the country's first foreign coach.<ref name="coachchanges">{{Cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A-%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F |script-title=bn:কোচ আসে কোচ যায়... |trans-title=Coaches comes, coaches goes... |work=Prothom Alo |language=bn |access-date=8 June 2023 |archive-date=8 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230608075405/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A-%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F |url-status=live}}</ref> The team faced internal conflict ahead of the [[Football at the 1978 Asian Games|1978 Asian Games]] in [[Bangkok]], as the captaincy initially handed to [[Monwar Hossain Nannu]] of [[Dhaka Abahani]] was eventually given to [[Shahidur Rahman Shantoo]] of [[Dhaka Derby|rival club]], [[Mohammedan SC (Dhaka)|Mohammedan SC]]. In protest, seven Abahani players, including Nannu, quit the squad.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-23710|title=The irreplaceable Nannu |date=17 February 2008 |work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]] |language=en |access-date=26 June 2019 |archive-date=20 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220920170440/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-23710|url-status=live}}</ref> The depleted and inexperienced team struggled in the tournament, losing 0–1 to [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]] and 0–3 to [[India men's national football team|India]].<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Erik|last1=Garin|first2=Karel|last2=Stokkermans|title=Asian Games 1978|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames78.html|access-date=20 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=20 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260420171932/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames78.html|url-status=live}}</ref> 

The [[1980 AFC Asian Cup qualification|1980 AFC Asian Cup qualifiers]] began on home soil from 1 March 1979, and under coaches Anwar Hossain and Anjam Hossain,<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh team Asian cup soccer|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=9|date=28 February 1979|language=en}}</ref> Bangladesh opened with draws against [[Afghanistan national football team|Afghanistan]]<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh hold Afghanistan 2–2|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=2 March 1979|language=en}}</ref> and [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]]<ref>{{cite news|title=Qatar hold Bangladesh|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=4|date=4 March 1979|language=en}}</ref> before securing a 3–2 victory over Afghanistan in the penultimate match,<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh record first victory|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=6 March 1979|language=en}}</ref> which was enough to qualify for the main tournament.<ref name="1980AFC">{{Cite web|first1=Neil|last1=Morrison|first2=Bojan|last2=Jovanovic|first3=Majeed|last3=Panahi|first4=Pieter|last4=Veroeveren|title=Asian Nations Cup 1980|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/80asch.html|access-date=20 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=20 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260420164505/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/80asch.html|url-status=live}}</ref> At the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup]] in [[Kuwait]], coached by the veteran, [[Abdur Rahim (footballer)|Abdur Rahim]],<ref>{{cite news|title=National soccer team leave for Kuwait today|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=6|date=13 September 1980|language=en}}</ref> Bangladesh were drawn with [[Iran national football team|Iran]], [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], [[Syria national football team|Syria]], and [[China national football team|China]]. They began the tournament respectably with a narrow 3–2 loss to North Korea,<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh go down fighting|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=6|date=18 September 1980|language=en}}</ref> with goals from Kazi Salahuddin and [[Ashraf Uddin Ahmed Chunnu|Ashrafuddin Ahmed Chunnu]],<ref name="closegames">{{Cite news|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8B-%E2%80%98%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B8%E2%80%99-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87|script-title=bn:যে ম্যাচগুলো 'আফসোস' বাংলাদেশের ফুটবলে|trans-title=Matches that are 'regrettable' in Bangladeshi football|first=Nair|last=Iqbal|work=Prothom Alo|language=bn|access-date=1 February 2022|archive-date=2 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230202061116/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9A%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8B-%E2%80%98%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%B8%E2%80%99-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87|url-status=live}}
</ref> and a 0–1 defeat to Syria.<ref>{{cite news|title=সিরিয়া জয়ী|trans-title=Syria wins|publisher=[[The Sangbad]]|page=3|date=22 September 1980|language=bn}}</ref> However, the remaining games saw Bangladesh suffer 7–0 and 6–0 respectively, to Iran and China,<ref>{{cite news|title=Iran defeat Bangladesh 7–0|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=6|date=24 September 1980|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=China dump Bangladesh 6–0|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=6|date=28 September 1980|language=en}}</ref> which saw them finish bottom of their group.<ref name="1980AFC"/>

===Shortcomings (1981–1989)===
[[File:Bangladesh national team at the 1984 South Asian Games in Nepal.png|thumb|right|Bangladesh at the [[Football at the 1984 South Asian Games|1984 SA Games]]|alt=File:Bangladesh national team at the 1984 South Asian Games in Nepal.png]]

Bangladesh took part in the [[1982 Quaid-e-Azam International Tournament]] in [[Karachi]], Pakistan, under the guidance of [[Abdul Gafur Baloch]], who had previously coached the country's youth teams.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A7%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B7%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AA|title=মুক্তিযুদ্ধে বাংলাদেশের পক্ষে থাকা এক পাকিস্তানির গল্প|trans-title=The story of a Pakistani who sided with Bangladesh in the Liberation War|language=bn|first=Rashedul|last=Islam|date=December 16, 2021|access-date=11 March 2024|website=[[Prothom Alo]]|archive-date=11 March 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240311210728/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A7%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B7%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%A5%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AA|url-status=live}}</ref> The team struggled throughout the competition, failing to win a single match and suffering a heavy 9–0 defeat to the [[Iran national football team|Iran]],<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Mark|last1=Cruickshank|first2=Neil|last2=Morrison|first3=Bojan|last3=Jovanovic|first4=Tom|last4=Lewis|first5=Karel|last5=Stokkermans|title=Quaid-e-Azam International Tournament, 1982 (Karachi, Pakistan)|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesq/quaid-e-azam.html#82|access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421094727/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesq/quaid-e-azam.html#82|url-status=live}}</ref> matching their loss to the [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] at the [[1979 President's Cup Football Tournament|1979 Korea President's Cup]] as their joint heaviest-ever defeat.<ref>{{cite news|title=South Korea beat Bangladesh 9–0|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=12|date=17 September 1979|language=en}}</ref> Following the tour, the BFF appointed German head coach Gerd Schmidt ahead of the [[Football at the 1982 Asian Games|1982 Asian Games]] in [[New Delhi]], India. Bangladesh went into the tournament without players from leading club [[Abahani Limited Dhaka|Dhaka Abahani]], who were arrested following a [[Dhaka First Division Football League|First Division League]] match brawl and were eventually indicted for allegedly conspiring in a potential coup against Chief Martial Law Administrator [[Hussain Muhammad Ershad]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kalerkantho.com/online/sport/2021/09/21/1075674|title=এরশাদের সামরিক আদালতে যেভাবে বিচার হয়েছিল সালাউদ্দিন-চুন্নুদের|trans-title=How Salahuddin and Chunnu were tried in Ershad's military court|language=bn|date=September 19, 2021|website=Kalerkantho|archive-date=3 January 2024|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20240103080124/https://www.kalerkantho.com/online/sport/2021/09/21/1075674|url-status=live|access-date=19 February 2023}}</ref> The team were eliminated at the group stage after defeats to [[India national football team|India]] and [[China national football team|China]], before earning their first victory on Asia's biggest stage against [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]] (2–1), with [[Ashish Bhadra]] and [[Badal Roy (footballer)|Badal Roy]] scoring.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A3%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A7%A9%E0%A7%A8-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%83%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF|script-title=bn:অনুপ্রেরণা ৩২ বছর আগের স্মৃতি|trans-title=Inspiration: Memories from 32 years ago|work=[[Prothom Alo]]|language=bn|access-date=2 February 2023|archive-date=2 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230202061119/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%A8%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A3%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A7%A9%E0%A7%A8-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%97%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%83%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 1983, during the [[1983 Bangladesh President's Gold Cup|third President's Gold Cup]] held in Dhaka, [[Ashrafuddin Ahmed Chunnu]] scored a hat-trick in a 4–2 victory over [[Nepal national football team|Nepal]] in a group match,<ref>{{cite news|title=লাল দলের সহজ জয় চুন্নুর হ্যাট্রিক|trans-title=Chunnu's hat-trick gives the Red team an easy win|publisher=[[The Sangbad]]|page=3|date=5 September 1983|language=bn}}</ref> becoming the first Bangladeshi player to achieve this feat.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.channelionline.com/%e0%a6%b8%e0%a6%ac%e0%a6%be%e0%a6%b0-%e0%a6%89%e0%a6%aa%e0%a6%b0%e0%a7%87-%e0%a6%86%e0%a6%b6%e0%a6%b0%e0%a6%be%e0%a6%ab%e0%a6%89%e0%a6%a6%e0%a7%8d%e0%a6%a6%e0%a6%bf%e0%a6%a8-%e0%a6%86%e0%a6%b9/|title=সবার উপরে আশরাফউদ্দিন আহমেদ চুন্নু|trans-title=Ashrafuddin Ahmed Chunnu is above everyone else|date=2020-07-05|access-date=2022-09-03|website=channelionline.com|archive-date=2020-10-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201026042819/https://www.channelionline.com/%e0%a6%b8%e0%a6%ac%e0%a6%be%e0%a6%b0-%e0%a6%89%e0%a6%aa%e0%a6%b0%e0%a7%87-%e0%a6%86%e0%a6%b6%e0%a6%b0%e0%a6%be%e0%a6%ab%e0%a6%89%e0%a6%a6%e0%a7%8d%e0%a6%a6%e0%a6%bf%e0%a6%a8-%e0%a6%86%e0%a6%b9/|url-status=live}}</ref> The following year, Bangladesh attempted to qualify for their second AFC Asian Cup but finished second from bottom in their group during the [[1984 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] in [[Jakarta]], Indonesia. In the inaugural [[South Asian Games]] in [[Football at the 1984 South Asian Games|1984]], Bangladesh participated without players from one of the country's leading clubs, [[Mohammedan SC (Dhaka)|Mohammedan SC]], who instead took part in the [[IFA Shield]] in India.<ref name="SAGameshist">{{Cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%A4%E0%A7%87-%E0%A7%A7%E0%A7%AB-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%B0...%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8 |script-title=bn:একটা সোনা জিততে ১৫ বছর...কেন?|trans-title=15 years to win a gold medal...why? |work=[[Prothom Alo]] |language=bn |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=20 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220920170719/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%8F%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE-%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%A4%E0%A7%87-%E0%A7%A7%E0%A7%AB-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%B0...%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8 |url-status=live}}</ref> The team topped the group stage, defeating [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] 2–0, [[Maldives national football team|Maldives]] 5–0, and Nepal 5–0.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Garin|first=Erik |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg84.html|title=1st South Asian Federation Games 1984|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=21 April 2026|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421145639/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg84.html|url-status=live}}</ref> However, they finished as runners-up after a 4–2 defeat to hosts Nepal in the final at the [[Dasharath Rangasala|Dasharath Stadium]] in [[Kathmandu]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.weeklyblitz.net/leisure/saff-games-gold-medal-an-illusion-for-the-bangladesh-team/ |title=SAFF Games gold medal, an illusion for the Bangladesh team |work=Weekly Blitz |date=2 May 2021 |access-date=4 January 2022 |archive-date=30 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211230145639/https://www.weeklyblitz.net/leisure/saff-games-gold-medal-an-illusion-for-the-bangladesh-team/ |url-status=live}}</ref>

In March 1985, Bangladesh began their [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|1986 FIFA World Cup qualification]] campaign, where they were grouped with India, [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]], and [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]].<ref name="1986WCQ">{{Cite web|first1=Karel|last1=Stokkermans|first2=Sergio Henrique|last2=Jarreta|title=World Cup 1986 Qualifying|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/86q.html|access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421151903/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/86q.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The team recorded its first-ever World Cup qualifying victory with a comeback win against Indonesia at the [[National Stadium, Dhaka|Dhaka Stadium]], overturning a one-goal deficit through goals from [[Kaiser Hamid]] and Ashrafuddin Ahmed Chunnu,<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh topple the top|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=3 April 1985|language=en}}</ref> and then followed it up with a 1–0 win over Thailand,<ref>{{cite news|title=Ilyas gem does it for Bangladesh|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=6 April 1985|language=en}}</ref> though they ultimately finished bottom of the group with four defeats.<ref name="1986WCQ"/>Following this, Bangladesh achieved a runners-up finish at the [[1985 Quaid-e-Azam International Tournament]] in [[Peshawar]], Pakistan, losing to [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]] in the final.<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Mark|last1=Cruickshank|first2=Neil|last2=Morrison|first3=Bojan|last3=Jovanovic|first4=Tom|last4=Lewis|first5=Karel|last5=Stokkermans|title=Quaid-E-Azam International Cup, 1985 (Peshawar, Pakistan)|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesq/quaid-e-azam.html#85 |access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421152854/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesq/quaid-e-azam.html#85|url-status=live}}</ref>  In the wake of their first World Cup qualifying campaign and strong showing in Pakistan, Bangladesh entered the [[Football at the 1985 South Asian Games|1985 South Asian Games]] in December as one of the favourites, topping their group, including an 8–0 victory over Maldives,<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh in SAF games soccer final|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=24 December 1985|language=en}}</ref> their biggest win ever, but fell short in the final, losing to India on penalties.<ref name="SAGameshist"/><ref>{{cite news|title=India win gold in tie-breaker|publisher=[[The Bangladesh Observer]]|page=1|date=26 December 1985|language=en}}</ref>

[[File:Iran vs Bangladesh, 1990 FIFA World Cup qualification.png|thumb|left|Bangladesh facing [[Iran national football team|Iran]] during their [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|1990 FIFA World Cup qualification]] fixture held in [[Tehran]] on 17 March 1989.|alt=File:Iran vs Bangladesh, 1990 FIFA World Cup qualification.png]]

In the [[Football at the 1986 Asian Games|1986 Asian Games]] in South Korea, Bangladesh suffered defeats to [[Japan national football team|Japan]], [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]], and Iran and won against Nepal, finishing second from bottom in their group.<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Erik|last1=Garin|first2=Bojan|last2=Jovanovic|first3=Neil|last3=Morrison|title=Asian Games 1986|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames86.html|access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421154021/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames86.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In the [[Football at the 1987 South Asian Games|1987 Sout Asian Games]], Bangladesh, who finished runners-up in the previous two editions, failed to reach the finals for the first time and lost the bronze medal match to [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]].<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Erik|last1=Garin|first2=Ian|last2=Kang|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg87.html|title=3rd South Asian Federation Games 1987 (Calcutta, India)|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=21 April 2026|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421154406/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg87.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In the [[1988 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], Bangladesh recorded three draws and two losses, failing to qualify from their group.<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Bojan|last1=Jovanovic|first2=Ian|last2=Kang|first3=Neil|last3=Morrison|first4=Majeed|last4=Panahi|first5=Pieter|last5=Veroeveren|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/88asch.html|title=Asian Nations Cup 1988|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=21 April 2026|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421154859/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/88asch.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In the [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|1990 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Bangladesh opened their campaign with a narrow 0–1 defeat to Thailand in [[Bangkok]]. In their next match, at the Dhaka Stadium, [[Rumman Bin Wali Sabbir]] missed a crucial penalty<ref name="closegames"/> as Bangladesh fell 2–1 to Iran in front of 10,879 spectators.<ref name="1990WCQ">{{Cite web|first=Barrie|last=Courtney|title=International Matches 1989 - Asia|url=https://www.rsssf.org/intldetails/1989as.html|access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421162434/https://www.rsssf.org/intldetails/1989as.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The team then suffered late defeats in the return fixtures against both Iran and China; notably, in [[Tehran]], before a crowd of 50,000, they conceded the deciding goal to [[Samad Marfavi]] in the 81st minute.<ref name="1990WCQ"/> Bangladesh's only victory came at home, where they defeated Thailand 3–1 at the Dhaka Stadium.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%93%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A6%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%AD%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E2%80%98%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A1%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E2%80%99|script-title=bn:সাব্বিরের ওপর যেদিন ভর করেছিলেন 'ম্যারাডোনা'|trans-title=The day 'Maradona' took over Sabbir|work=[[Prothom Alo]]|language=bn|access-date=13 August 2021|archive-date=13 August 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210813152019/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%93%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%AF%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A6%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%AD%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E2%80%98%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A1%E0%A7%8B%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E2%80%99|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|first1=Karel|last1=Stokkermans|first2=Sergio Henrique|last2=Jarreta|title=World Cup 1990 Qualifying|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/90q.html|access-date=21 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=21 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260421161744/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/90q.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Bangladesh won the [[1989 Bangladesh President's Gold Cup|1989 President's Gold Cup]] in Dhaka, defeating [[Korea University]] 4–3 on penalties at [[Mirpur Stadium]]. Notably, Bangladesh was the only full national team to participate in that edition aside from India.<ref name="GoldCup989">{{Cite web |url=https://www.utpalshuvro.com/special-article/news/1073 |script-title=bn:লাল দলের রাঙিয়ে দেওয়া প্রেসিডেন্ট গোল্ড কাপ ফুটবল |website=Utpal Shuvro |language=bn |access-date=24 March 2022 |archive-date=17 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220217113847/https://www.utpalshuvro.com/special-article/news/1073 |url-status=live}}</ref> Iranian [[Nasser Hejazi]] took charge of Bangladesh for the [[Football at the 1989 South Asian Games|1989 South Asian Games]] in [[Islamabad]], Pakistan, and controversially dropped experienced players [[Sheikh Mohammad Aslam]] and [[Khandoker Wasim Iqbal|Wasim Iqbal]] due to disciplinary issues.<ref name="coachchanges"/> Bangladesh began the tournament with a convincing 3–0 victory over [[Sri Lanka national football team|Sri Lanka]] and secured a place in the gold medal match after a 1–1 draw against India. However, the team finished as runners-up after losing 1–0 to Pakistan in the final.<ref name="SAGameshist"/> Following the tournament, the BFF imposed suspensions on several players, including captain [[Elias Hossain]], effectively ending his international career.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AD%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A4-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6-%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%A7%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%B6%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF|date=9 April 2020|script-title=bn:ভারত পেল পেনাল্টি, বাংলাদেশ অধিনায়ক পেলেন শাস্তি|trans-title=India gets the penalty, Bangladesh captain gets punished|website=[[Prothom Alo]]|access-date=4 January 2024|archive-date=4 January 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240104111931/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%AD%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%A4-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%9F%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%82%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B6-%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%A7%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A7%9F%E0%A6%95-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%B6%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B8%E0%A7%8D%E0%A6%A4%E0%A6%BF|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Mixed results (1990–2000)===
Bangladesh began the decade by participating in the [[Football at the 1990 Asian Games – Men's tournament|1990 Asian Games]] in [[Beijing]], China, where they lost both of their group-stage matches, 0–4 to [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] and 0–3 to [[Japan national football team|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Erik|last1=Garin|first2=Bojan|last2=Jovanovic|title=Asian Games 1990|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames90.html|access-date=22 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=22 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260422155428/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames90.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In December 1991, they competed in the [[Football at the 1991 South Asian Games|1991 South Asian Games]] in [[Colombo]], Sri Lanka, opening with a 0–1 defeat to [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]], their third consecutive loss to their rivals. They recovered in the next match with their first-ever victory against [[India national football team|India]], winning 2–1 thanks to a brace from [[Rizvi Karim Rumi]],<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%9C-%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%B9%E0%A6%A4%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%89 |script-title=bn:আজ রুমি হতে পারবেন কেউ? |trans-title=Can anyone be Rumi today? |work=[[Prothom Alo]] |language=bn |access-date=3 January 2022 |archive-date=3 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220103183708/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%9C-%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BF-%E0%A6%B9%E0%A6%A4%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%AA%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%95%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%89 |url-status=live}}</ref> and finished second in their group to qualify for the bronze-medal match, where they defeated [[Nepal national football team|Nepal]] 2–0.<ref>{{cite web|first=Josef|last=Bobrowsky|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg91.html|title=5th South Asian Federation Games 1991 (Colombo, Sri Lanka)|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=16 May 2020 |archive-date=22 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260422160255/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg91.html |url-status=live}}</ref> The following year, in the [[1992 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] in [[Bangkok]], Thailand, Bangladesh finished bottom of their group after losing 0–6 to [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] and 0–1 to [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]].<ref>{{cite web|first1=Bojan|last1=Jovanovic|first2=Ian|last2=King|first3=Majeed|last3=Panahi|first4=Josef|last4=Bobrowsky|first5=Pieter|last5=Veroeveren|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/92asch.html |title=Asian Nations Cup 1992 |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=22 April 2026 |archive-date=22 April 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260422160627/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/92asch.html |url-status=live}}</ref>

In April 1993, Bangladesh began their participation in the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round]] under coach, [[Kazi Salahuddin]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.deshrupantor.com/rupantorer-bangladesh-3/2020/01/22/194455 |script-title=bn:বাংলাদেশ ফুটবলের প্রথম মেগাস্টার |trans-title=The first megastar of Bangladesh football |website=Desh Rupantor |language=bn |access-date=29 September 2022 |archive-date=29 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220929101738/https://www.deshrupantor.com/rupantorer-bangladesh-3/2020/01/22/194455 |url-status=live}}</ref>  In their first match at the [[National Stadium (Tokyo)|Tokyo National Stadium]], Bangladesh were defeated 0–8 by hosts, Japan. The team performed in the same trend throughout the qualifiers, losing by big margins against both Thailand and [[United Arab Emirates national football team|UAE]] over two fixtures, and also losing 1–4 to Japan in the reverse fixture. Bangladesh's only victories came against [[Sri Lanka national football team|Sri Lanka]] winning 1–0 and 3–0 during their encounters, as the team finished second from bottom in their group.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Stokkermans |first1=Karel |last2=Henrique Jarreta |first2=Sergio |title=World Cup 1994 Qualifying |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/94q.html |publisher=[[RSSSF]] |date=30 December 2019 |access-date=18 June 2024 |archive-date=22 April 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260422162539/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/94q.html |url-status=live}}</ref> Ahead of the [[Football at the 1993 South Asian Games|1993 South Asian Games]] held on home soil, the BFF appointed Swiss coach, [[Oldřich Šváb]].<ref name="coachchanges"/> Bangladesh responded with their worst finish in the tournament, finishing bottom of their group.<ref>{{Cite web|first=Neil|last=Morrison|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg93.html|title=6th South Asian Federation Games 1993|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=13 May 2024|archive-date=22 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260422162913/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg93.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[File:Bangladesh's first international trophy.jpg|right|thumb|Bangladesh celebrating their [[Burma Tournament 1995|4-nation Tiger Trophy]] triumph in Myanmar, 1995.]]

{{football squad on pitch|align=right|image=striped
| GK  = '''[[Biplob Bhattacharjee|Biplob]]'''
| RB  = '''[[Abu Ahmed Faysal|Faysal]]''' 
| RCB = '''[[Kazi Nazrul Islam (footballer)|Nazrul]]'''
| LCB = '''[[Mohammed Jewel Rana|Jewel]] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])'''
| LB  = '''[[Hassan Al-Mamun|Mamun]]'''
| RCM = '''[[Iqbal Hossain (footballer)|Iqbal]]'''
| LCM = '''[[Motiur Rahman Munna|Munna]]'''
| AM = '''[[Mohammed Monwar Hossain|Monwar]]'''
| RW = '''[[Alfaz Ahmed|Alfaz]]'''
| CF = '''[[Mizanur Rahman Dawn|Dawn]]'''
| LW = '''[[Anwar Hossain (footballer)|Anwar]]'''
| caption = Bangladesh's starting line-up in the [[Football at the 1999 South Asian Games|1999 South Asian Games]] final.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.utpalshuvro.com/photo-feature/news/158|script-title=bn:সাফ ফুটবলে প্রথম সোনাজয়ী দল|website=Utpal Shuvro|trans-title=The first gold-winning team in SAFF Football|language=bn|access-date=28 September 2022|archive-date=21 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220921225403/https://www.utpalshuvro.com/photo-feature/news/158|url-status=live}}</ref>
}}

In March 1995, after missing the inaugural edition, Bangladesh participated in the [[1995 SAARC Gold Cup]] in Colombo under coach [[Kang Man-young]].<ref name="coachchanges"/> They opened the tournament with their fourth successive defeat to Pakistan but rebounded with a 2–0 win over Nepal to reach the semi-finals, where they were eliminated by India on penalties at the [[Sugathadasa Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web|first=Josef|last=Bobrowsky|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold95.html|title=South Asian Gold Cup 1995 (Colombo, Sri Lanka)|access-date=26 April 2026|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426091336/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold95.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In October 1995, German coach [[Otto Pfister]] took charge of the team, and from 26 October to 4 November 1995, he led them in the [[Burma Tournament 1995|4-nation Tiger Trophy]] in [[Yangon]]. Under the captaincy of [[Monem Munna]], Bangladesh defeated hosts [[Myanmar national football team|Myanmar]] 2–1 in the final, with goals from [[Mamun Joarder]] and [[Imtiaz Ahmed Nakib]],<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myanmar stunned 1-2 by Bangladesh The Straits Times, 5 November 1995, Page 32|url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitstimes19951105-1.2.48.2?qt=myanmar,%20quadrangular&q=myanmar%20quadrangular%20|access-date=2025-01-17|website=eresources.nlb.gov.sg|archive-date=2026-04-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426092021/https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitstimes19951105-1.2.48.2?qt=myanmar,%20quadrangular&q=myanmar%20quadrangular%20|url-status=live}}</ref> securing the country's first international trophy. In December, the team competed in the [[Football at the 1995 South Asian Games|1995 South Asian Games]] in [[Chennai|Madras]], India, and lost 1–0 in the final to the hosts, earning their fourth silver medal and extending their 15-year run without a gold.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://en.prothomalo.com/sports/football/bangladesh-would-play-better-than-india |title=Bangladesh would play better than India |first=Rashedul |last=Islam |work=[[Prothom Alo]] |access-date=4 January 2022 |archive-date=31 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211231110947/https://en.prothomalo.com/sports/football/bangladesh-would-play-better-than-india |url-status=live}}</ref>

{{multiple image
| align             = left
| direction         = vertical
| image1            = Bangladesh and India in action at the 1999 South Asian Games semi-final.png
| width1            = 
| image2            = Bangladesh national football team players celebrating following their triumph in the 1999 South Asian Games final.png
| footer            = Scenes during the [[Football at the 1999 South Asian Games|1999 South Asian Games]]: against [[India national football team|India]] in the semi-final (above); and  after Bangladesh secured their maiden gold medal following triumph over hosts, [[Nepal national football team|Nepal]], in the final (below).
| total_width       = 
| width2            = 
| caption1          = 
| caption2          = 
}}

In 1996, Bangladesh reached their highest-ever [[FIFA Men's World Ranking|FIFA ranking]] of 110; nevertheless, the team remained inactive throughout the year and withdrew from the [[1996 AFC Asian Cup qualification]].<ref>{{Cite web|first=Karel|last=Stokkermans|title=Asian Games 1990|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/96asch.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426095045/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/96asch.html|url-status=live}}</ref> From 16 to 31 March 1997, they took part in the [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round]], held jointly in [[Shah Alam]], Malaysia and [[Jeddah]], Saudi Arabia. Bangladesh finished bottom of their group, with their only victory being a 2–1 win over [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]].<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Karel|last1=Stokkermans|first2=Sergio Henrique|last2=Jarreta|title=World Cup 1998 Qualifying|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98q.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426100320/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98q.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Pfister's departure was eventually confirmed later that year, following the ''Red and Green's'' failure to progress beyond the group stage of the [[1997 SAFF Gold Cup]] in September.<ref>{{Cite web|first=Arunava|last=Chaudhuri|title=South Asian Gold Cup 1997 (Kathmandu, Nepal)|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold97.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426100712/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold97.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In October 1998, former Iraqi international [[Samir Shaker]] was appointed head coach, and his first assignment came in the [[1999 SAFF Gold Cup]] in [[Goa]], India, held from 22 April to 1 May 1997. Bangladesh topped their group after a 0–0 draw against India and a 4–0 win over Pakistan, their first victory over the neighbours in 13 years. They then defeated Nepal 2–1 in the semi-finals to reach the tournament final for the first time; however, the team lost 0–2 to [[Baichung Bhutia]] led India in the final at the [[Jawaharlal Nehru Stadium (Margao)|Fatorda Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite web|first=Arunava|last=Chaudhuri|title=South Asian Gold Cup 1999 (Margoa, Goa)|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold99.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426134826/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/saffgold99.html|url-status=live}}</ref> 

From 24 September to 4 October 1999, Bangladesh competed in the [[Football at the 1999 South Asian Games|1999 South Asian Games]] in [[Kathmandu]], Nepal. Led by captain [[Mohammed Jewel Rana|Jewel Rana]], they opened with a 1–2 loss to the Maldives but topped their group after a 1–0 win over Sri Lanka in the next match.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.utpalshuvro.com/special-article/news/1328|script-title=bn:যে শিরোপার অপেক্ষায় ছিল বাংলাদেশ|trans-title=The title that Bangladesh was waiting for|website=Utpal Shuvro|date=24 September 2021|access-date=28 February 2025|archive-date=9 October 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231009165610/https://www.utpalshuvro.com/special-article/news/1328|url-status=live}}</ref> In the semi-final against India, [[Shahajuddin Tipu]] scored the only goal to secure the country's second-ever victory against the regional giants.<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh in soccer final|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|page=1|date=3 October 1999|language=en}}</ref> In the final, held at the [[Dasharath Stadium]] on 4 October 1999, [[Alfaz Ahmed]] scored the decisive goal as Bangladesh defeated hosts Nepal 1–0 to end their 19-year, eight-edition wait for a gold medal.<ref>{{Cite news |first=Nair|last=Iqbal|url=https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%B6%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%9A%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%96%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%96%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9C |script-title=bn:সামির শাকিরের চোখে জল দেখেছিলেন আলফাজ |trans-title=Alfaz saw tears in Samir Shakir's eyes |work=[[Prothom Alo]] |language=bn |access-date=8 June 2023 |archive-date=8 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230608083952/https://www.prothomalo.com/sports/football/%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%B6%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0-%E0%A6%9A%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%96%E0%A7%87-%E0%A6%9C%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%A6%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%96%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%9B%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%A8-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%B2%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9C |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Gold glitters for booters, finally|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|page=1|date=5 October 1999|language=en}}</ref>

In November 1999, the team concluded the millennium by participating in the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] held in [[Abu Dhabi]], UAE, failing to progress, losing 0–6	against [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]], 0–3 to UAE, defeating Sri Lanka 3–1 and finishing the qualifiers with a 2–2 draw against India,<ref>{{Cite web|first=Karel|last=Stokkermans|title=Asian Nations Cup 2000|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/00asch.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426135716/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/00asch.html|url-status=live}}</ref> after which Shaker departed, as one of the most successful coaches in the country's history, due to coaching license issues.<ref name="coachchanges"/> In July 2000, Bangladesh played two friendly matches in [[England]]. Their first match was against [[Football League First Division|English Division One]] team [[Huddersfield Town A.F.C.|Huddersfield Town]] on 26 July in [[Birmingham]], which ended in a 5–0 defeat.<ref>{{cite news|newspaper=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|date=28 July 2000|title=BFF, please explain|page=13}}</ref> The team then suffered a defeat in their final friendly against India, losing 1–0 in a match held at [[Filbert Street]] in [[Leicester]] on 29 July.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dhakatribune.com/football/2022/11/24/a-subcontinental-affair-in-london|title=A subcontinental affair in London|date=November 24, 2022|website=dhakatribune.com|archive-date=19 November 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231119180630/https://www.dhakatribune.com/sport/football/298906/a-subcontinental-affair-in-london|url-status=live}}</ref> The Bangladesh team was reportedly paid an appearance fee of [[Pound sterling|£]]10,000 for participating in these matches, marking the team's first European tour.<ref>{{cite news|newspaper=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|date=21 July 2000|title=Booters off to England|page=8}}</ref>

===FIFA suspension and aftermath (2001–2010)===
On 12 January 2001, Bangladesh played their first match against European opposition, facing [[Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team|Bosnia & Herzegovina]] in the [[Millennium Super Soccer Cup|Sahara Cup]] in India, where they lost 2–0.<ref>{{cite news|title=Bangladesh slip in second half|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|page=15|date=13 January 2001|language=en}}</ref> On 16 January, [[Firoj Mahmud Titu]] became the first Bangladeshi player to score against European opposition in a 4–1 defeat to [[Serbia and Montenegro national football team|FR Yugoslavia]] in the same competition.<ref>{{cite news|title=যুগোস্লাভিয়া কাছে ১-৪ গোলে হেরেছে বাংলাদেশ|trans-title=Bangladesh lost 1-4 to Yugoslavia|publisher=[[Prothom Alo]]|page=10|date=17 January 2001|language=bn}}</ref> The team's results declined further, and they were eliminated in the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|first round]] of the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2002 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], winning one of four matches.<ref>{{Cite web|first=Karel|last=Stokkermans|title=World Cup 2002 Qualifying|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/2002q.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426143655/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/2002q.html|url-status=live}}</ref> On 11 January 2002, FIFA imposed a ban on [[Bangladesh Football Federation|Bangladesh]] due to government interference in the Bangladesh Football Federation, which violated FIFA and AFC statutes requiring a democratically elected committee.<ref>{{cite news|title=FIFA bans Bangladesh|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|page=1|date=12 January 2002|language=en}}</ref> The ban was lifted on 11 February after the government reinstated the elected committee or BFF.<ref>{{cite news|title=FIFA lifts ban on Bangladesh|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|page=1|date=12 February 2002|language=en}}</ref>

{{football squad on pitch|align=right|image=striped
| GK_nat  = | GK  = '''[[Aminul Haque (footballer)|Aminul]]'''
| RB_nat  = | RB  = '''[[Firoj Mahmud Titu|Titu]]'''
| RCB_nat = | RCB = '''[[Kazi Nazrul Islam (footballer)|Nazrul]]'''
| CB_nat =  | CB   = '''[[Mohammed Sujan|Sujan]]'''
| LCB_nat = | LCB = '''[[Hassan Al-Mamun|Mamun]] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])'''
| LB_nat  = | LB  = '''[[Mustafa Anwar Parvez Babu|Parvez]]'''
| LCM_nat = | RCM = '''[[Arif Khan Joy|Joy]]'''
| RCM_nat = | LCM = '''[[Motiur Rahman Munna|Munna]]'''
| AM_nat  = | AM  = '''[[Arman Mia|Arman]]'''
| RCF_nat = | RCF = '''[[Alfaz Ahmed|Alfaz]]'''
| LCF_nat = | LCF = '''[[Rokonuzzaman Kanchan|Kanchan]]'''
| caption = [[2003 SAFF Gold Cup|2003 South Asian Football Federation Gold Cup]] final starting line-up.
}}

The [[2003 SAFF Gold Cup]], originally scheduled for 2002 but delayed a year due to the suspension of the BFF by FIFA, took place in [[Dhaka]] from 10 to 20 January 2003. Bangladesh finished top of their group to advance to the semi-final. They subsequently defeated defending champions [[India national football team|India]] 2–1, with [[Motiur Rahman Munna|Motiur Munna]] scoring a [[golden goal]] in [[extra time]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news/matiur-magic-sees-hosts-through-to-saff-final|title=Matiur magic sees hosts through to SAFF final|date=January 19, 2003|access-date=2 January 2025|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=2 January 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250102115022/https://www.thedailystar.net/news/matiur-magic-sees-hosts-through-to-saff-final|url-status=live}}</ref> In the final, [[Hassan Al-Mamun]] replaced the suspended [[Rajani Kanta Barman]] as captain. Bangladesh drew 1–1 with Maldives after extra time, with [[Rokonuzzaman Kanchan]] scoring and [[Ali Umar]] equalising. Bangladesh won the penalty shootout 4–3, with [[Aminul Haque (footballer)|Aminul Haque]] saving a penalty and [[Mohammed Sujan]] scoring the decisive kick, securing their first SAFF Championship.The victory marked the end of [[György Kottán]]'s tenure as head coach, whose contract expired after the tournament, he had served since November 2000.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news/new-saff-kings|title=New SAFF kings|access-date=15 January 2024|date=21 January 2003|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=24 March 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240324140449/https://www.thedailystar.net/news/new-saff-kings|url-status=live}}</ref>

The [[2004 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] began in March 2003. The team under newly-appointed coach [[Hasanuzzaman Khan Bablu]] failed to qualify for the competition after suffering a defeat to [[Laos national football team|Laos]] and a draw against [[Hong Kong national football team|Hong Kong]].<ref>{{Cite web|first1=Sturmius|last1=Burkert|first2=Glenn|last2=Cowlam|first3=Julián|last3=Díaz Rubio|first4=Refel|last4=Hashim|first5=Seungsoo|last5=Lee|first6=Malik Riaz Hai|last6=Naveed|first7=Hamdan|last7=Saaid|title=Asian Nations Cup 2004|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/04asch.html|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[RSSSF]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426151025/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/04asch.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In November, during [[Golam Sarwar Tipu]]'s fourth and final stint as head coach,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2003/11/22/d31122040131.htm|title=Poorly prepared BD face Tajikistan Nov 26|access-date=26 April 2026|date=22 November 2003|website=The Daily Star|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426151343/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2003/11/22/d31122040131.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh lost 4–0 on aggregate against [[Tajikistan national football team|Tajikistan]] in the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round]], which resulted in their absence from international football for the entirety of 2004.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2003/12/01/d31201040640.htm|title=Again 2-0 loss to Tajikistan|date=1 December 2003|access-date=26 April 2026|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=26 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260426150619/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2003/12/01/d31201040640.htm|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[File:Bangladesh football team group photo during the 2005 SAFF Cup.png|left|thumb|Bangladesh squad that took part in the [[2005 SAFF Gold Cup]] in [[Karachi]], Pakistan.]]

In August 2005, the BFF appointed Argentine head coach [[Andrés Cruciani|Diego Andrés Cruciani]] on a one-year contract.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/08/05/d50805040230.htm|title=BFF promises Cruciani a free hand|access-date=27 April 2026|date=5 August 2005|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427130654/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/08/05/d50805040230.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> Cruciani led Bangladesh to the final of the [[2005 SAFF Gold Cup]] in [[Karachi]], Pakistan, where they lost to India by 2–0.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/12/18/d51218040222.htm|title=It was one of those rare off-days: Cruciani|access-date=27 April 2026|date=18 December 2005|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427131132/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/12/18/d51218040222.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> In December 2005, the team participated in the preliminary round of the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], defeating Pakistan 1–0 on aggregate to advance to the final qualifiers.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/12/27/d51227011410.htm|title=Bangladesh move to qualifying stage|access-date=27 April 2026|date=27 December 2005|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427131720/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2005/12/27/d51227011410.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> 

Admist their Asian Cup qualification campaign, Bangladesh hosted the [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup]], and reached the quarter-finals, where they were defeated 6–1 by Tajikistan.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/04/11/d60411040333.htm|title=Booters crash out|access-date=27 April 2026|date=11 April 2006|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427132149/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/04/11/d60411040333.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> Cruciani was eventually removed, following three consecutive losses in the final round of the 2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification. Interim coach Hasanuzzaman Bablu took charge for the remaining qualification matches,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/10/11/d61011040228.htm|title=Uzbek test for Bangladesh|access-date=27 April 2026|date=11 October 2006|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427132909/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/10/11/d61011040228.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> with Bangladesh losing all four games and finishing bottom of their group.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/11/16/d61116040133.htm|title=No solace for Bangladesh|access-date=27 April 2026|date=16 November 2006|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427133122/https://archive.thedailystar.net/2006/11/16/d61116040133.htm|url-status=live}}</ref>

The team participated in the [[2007 Nehru Cup]] in [[New Delhi]], India, with Indian coach [[Syed Nayeemuddin]], and finished second from the bottom in their group.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-1371|title=Bangladesh routed|access-date=27 April 2026|date=24 August 2007|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427133710/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-1371|url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh was also eliminated in the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|first round]] of the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2010 FIFA World Cup qualification]], losing 6–1 on aggregate to Tajikistan,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://bdnews24.com/sport/bangladesh-lose-to-tajikistan-5-0-in-wc-pre-qualifier|title=Bangladesh lose to Tajikistan 5-0 in WC pre-qualifier|access-date=27 April 2026|date=28 October 2007|website=[[Bdnews24.com]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427134333/https://bdnews24.com/sport/bangladesh-lose-to-tajikistan-5-0-in-wc-pre-qualifier|url-status=live}}</ref> leading to Nayeemuddin's dismissal.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archive.thedailystar.net/newDesign/cache/cached-news-details-13082.html|date=26 November 2007|title=Farewell to Nayeem|access-date=20 January 2025|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=20 January 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250120111037/https://archive.thedailystar.net/newDesign/cache/cached-news-details-13082.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh participated in the [[2008 AFC Challenge Cup qualification]] and the [[2008 SAFF Championship]], with coach [[Mohammad Abu Yusuf|Abu Yusuf]], and remained winless.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-41620|title=Yusuf wants to leave|date=June 18, 2008|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=25 November 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231125081330/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-41620|url-status=live}}</ref>

Bangladesh continued their winless run following participating in the [[2008 Merdeka Tournament|2008 Merdeka Cup]] and the [[2008 Myanmar Grand Royal Challenge Cup|2008 Myanmar Challenge Cup]], during [[Shafiqul Islam Manik]]'s tenure.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-64383|title=The future is there|date=21 November 2008|access-date=27 April 2026|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427140927/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-6775|url-status=live}}</ref>  Under Brazilian coach [[Dido (footballer)|Dido]], Bangladesh secured qualification for the [[2010 AFC Challenge Cup]], and during the qualifiers, their 2–1 victory over [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] on 26 April 2009 ended a winless streak that had lasted over three years.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-85785|title=Hosts ride on Enamul|date=April 27, 2009|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|archive-date=September 13, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230913115616/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-85785|url-status=live}}</ref> Dido was sacked on 9 November, citing interference from the BFF in squad selection and internal team conflicts.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-115841|title=Salahuddin is surrounded by sharks|first=Quazi Zulquarnain|last=Islam|date=30 November 2009|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=19 December 2021|archive-date=16 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216072953/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-115841|url-status=live}}</ref> Interim coach [[Shahidur Rahman Shantoo]] led Bangladesh to a semi-final elimination to [[India national under-23 football team|India U23]] in the [[2009 SAFF Championship]] in Dhaka.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-117441|title=Hosts have no answers|date=December 11, 2009|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=December 8, 2022|archive-date=December 19, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211219111455/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-117441|url-status=live}}</ref> In 2010, under interim [[Saiful Bari Titu]], the team was eliminated in the group stage of the AFC Challenge Cup held in [[Colombo]], Sri Lanka.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-127252|title=Booters' tame exit|date=20 February 2010|website=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427142036/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-127252|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Decline (2011–2017)===
Bangladesh failed to qualify for the [[2012 AFC Challenge Cup]], under Croatian coach [[Robert Rubčić]], leading to his dismissal. The BFF appointed Macedonian coach [[Nikola Ilievski]] just four days before the country's [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round]] match against the [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]] in [[Dhaka]] on 29 June 2011.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-191543|title=Ilievski chapter begins|date=26 June 2011|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=10 January 2022|archive-date=10 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220110113844/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-191543 |url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh won the first leg 3–0 on a waterlogged pitch at [[Bangabandhu National Stadium]]<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-192122|title=Bangladesh drown Pakistan in rain|date=29 June 2011|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427151649/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-192122|url-status=live}}</ref> and advanced to the second round after a goalless draw in [[Lahore]] four days later.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.dawn.com/news/641258/draw-against-bangladesh-throws-pakistan-out-of-wc-qualifiers|title=Draw against Bangladesh throws Pakistan out of WC qualifiers|date=3 July 2011|work=[[Dawn (newspaper)|Dawn]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427151836/https://www.dawn.com/news/641258/draw-against-bangladesh-throws-pakistan-out-of-wc-qualifiers|url-status=live}}</ref> Drawn against [[Lebanon national football team|Lebanon]] in the second round, Bangladesh lost the first leg 4–0 in [[Beirut]]. Despite being eliminated, they secured a 2–0 victory in the return leg in Dhaka.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-196214|title=Too good, but too late|date=29 July 2011|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=19 December 2021|archive-date=19 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211219113848/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-196214|url-status=live}}</ref> Ilievski's tenure ended following a group-stage exit from the [[2011 SAFF Championship]] in [[New Delhi]], India.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-213247|title=Bangladesh hit new low|date=8 December 2011|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=14 September 2023|archive-date=14 September 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230914081431/https://www.thedailystar.net/news-detail-213247|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2013, the team competed in [[2014 AFC Challenge Cup qualification]] under Dutch coach [[Lodewijk de Kruif]], narrowly missing qualification.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news/yet-de-kruif-dissatisfied|title=Yet de Kruif dissatisfied|date=6 March 2013|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427152422/https://www.thedailystar.net/news/yet-de-kruif-dissatisfied|url-status=live}}</ref> De Kruif remained in charge for the [[2013 SAFF Championship]] in [[Kathmandu]], Nepal, where Bangladesh were eliminated after finishing bottom of their group.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/news/bangladesh-bow-out|title=Bangladesh bow out|date=5 September 2013|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427152647/https://www.thedailystar.net/news/bangladesh-bow-out|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[File:Bangladesh Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina Meets Bangladesh National Football Team February 8 2015 (PID-0060717).jpg|thumb|[[2015 Bangabandhu Cup]] runner-up Bangladesh team feeted by [[Prime Minister of Bangladesh|Prime Minister]] [[Sheikh Hasina]] (standing middle, top row).]]

De Kruif temporarily departed in October 2014, before returning<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/de-kruif-continue-99496|title=De Kruif to continue|date=18 June 2015|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427154600/https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/de-kruif-continue-99496|url-status=live}}</ref> to lead Bangladesh to a runner-up finish in the [[2015 Bangabandhu Cup]] held in Dhaka, where they suffered a 3–2 defeat to [[Malaysia national under-23 football team|Malaysia Olympic team]] in the final.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://online.thedailystar.net/sports/football/we-love-tragic-heroes-1519|title=We love tragic heroes|date=9 February 2015|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427155327/https://online.thedailystar.net/sports/football/we-love-tragic-heroes-1519|url-status=live}}</ref> In June 2015, Bangladesh began their [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2018 FIFA World Cup qualification]] campaign in the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round]], where they were drawn into a group with [[Kyrgyzstan national football team|Kyrgyzstan]], [[Tajikistan national football team|Tajikistan]], [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] and [[Jordan national football team|Jordan]]. The team suffered heavy defeats in all but one of their first four matches, managing a 1–1 draw against Tajikistan at the Bangabandhu National Stadium. De Kruif was subsequently replaced by Italian coach [[Fabio Lopez]],<ref>{{cite web|url=https://bdnews24.com/sport/italy-s-fabio-lopez-replaces-lodewijk-de-kruif-as-bangladesh-football-coach|title=Italy's Fabio Lopez replaces Lodewijk de Kruif as Bangladesh football coach|access-date=27 April 2026|date=11 September 2015|website=[[Bdnews24.com]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427160626/https://bdnews24.com/sport/italy-s-fabio-lopez-replaces-lodewijk-de-kruif-as-bangladesh-football-coach|url-status=live}}</ref> under whom Bangladesh lost their next three matches, leading to his dismissal.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.dhakatribune.com/sport/football/114405/lopez-sacked|title=Lopez sacked|access-date=27 April 2026|date=24 November 2015|website=[[Dhaka Tribune]]|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427160810/https://www.dhakatribune.com/sport/football/114405/lopez-sacked|url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh, under [[Maruful Haque]], also exited the [[2015 SAFF Championship]] from the group-stage.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/maruful-paints-sorry-picture-193705|title=Maruful paints a sorry picture|date=28 December 2015 |work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427161119/https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/maruful-paints-sorry-picture-193705|url-status=live}}</ref> On 24 March 2016, the team played their final World Cup qualification match under Spanish coach [[Gonzalo Sánchez Moreno|Gonzalo Moreno]], losing 8–0 to Jordan at the [[Amman International Stadium]] in [[Amman]], Jordan, and finishing at the bottom of Group B.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/booters-suffer-massive-defeat-1199254|title=Booters suffer massive defeat|date=25 March 2016|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 June 2022|archive-date=27 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220627141032/https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/booters-suffer-massive-defeat-1199254|url-status=live}}</ref>

In May 2016, De Kruif was reappointed as head coach to guide Bangladesh through the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification – play-off round]] against Tajikistan.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/de-kruif-coming-today-1224934|title=De Kruif coming today|date=16 May 2016|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427162731/https://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/de-kruif-coming-today-1224934|url-status=live}}</ref> Bangladesh lost the play-offs 6–0 on aggregate, and were relegated to the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification – play-off round|second round]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://online.thedailystar.net/sports/football/narrow-defeat-time-1235959|title=Narrow defeat this time|date=8 June 2016|work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427162934/https://online.thedailystar.net/sports/football/narrow-defeat-time-1235959|url-status=live}}</ref> On 29 June, the BFF appointed Belgian coach [[Tom Saintfiet]] on a short-term contract to oversee the team for the second-round matches against [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://bdnews24.com/sport/bangladesh-appoints-belgium-s-tom-saintfiet-as-head-football-coach|title=Bangladesh appoints Belgium's Tom Saintfiet as head football coach|date=12 July 2016|work=[[Bdnews24.com]]|access-date=27 April 2026|archive-date=27 April 2026|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260427163206/https://bdnews24.com/sport/bangladesh-appoints-belgium-s-tom-saintfiet-as-head-football-coach|url-status=live}}</ref> After a 0–0 draw in the first leg in Dhaka, Bangladesh were defeated 3–1 by Bhutan in the second leg at the [[Changlimithang Stadium]] in [[Thimphu]]. The result meant Bangladesh would not be a part of any AFC and FIFA organised tournaments for the following two years.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/humiliation-bhutan-1297420 |title=Humiliation in Bhutan |work=[[The Daily Star (Bangladesh)|The Daily Star]]|date=11 October 2016 |access-date=20 October 2016 |archive-date=21 October 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161021064213/http://www.thedailystar.net/sports/football/humiliation-bhutan-1297420 |url-status=live}}</ref> The team was absent from international football throughout 2017 and by February 2018, had dropped to 197th place in the [[FIFA Men's World Ranking]], their all-time lowest.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.newagebd.net/article/33117/bangladesh-football-fails-to-live-up-to-dream |title=Bangladesh football fails to live up to dream |work=[[New Age (Bangladesh)|New Age]] |access-date=9 December 2021 |archive-date=9 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211209104237/https://www.newagebd.net/article/33117/bangladesh-football-fails-to-live-up-to-dream |url-status=live}}</ref>

===Recent years (2018–present)===
